summary: Charity Majors argues that no amount of extracurricular contributions — interviewing, mentoring, speaking, community building — can compensate for failing to deliver on your core job responsibilities. She illustrates through a story how high-visibility side work actually becomes evidence against you when your primary deliverables are slipping. The post urges engineers to ruthlessly prioritize their 'One Job' first, and to honestly reassess whether they're in the right role if they chronically can't find motivation for it.
key_points: Your core job responsibilities always take priority over extracurricular contributions
Extras become strikes against you when your primary work is slipping
Feeling overwhelmed can make all commitments feel equal weight, but they aren't
Chronic inability to motivate on core work may signal you're in the wrong role
Core responsibilities should never add up to 40+ hours per week
money_quotes: When someone isn't meeting the basic expectations for their core responsibilities, it doesn't matter how many other wonderful things they are doing. In fact, those things can become strikes *against* them.

When you are meeting expectations for your One Job — and you don't necessarily have to be dazzling, just competent and predictable — then picking up other work is a sign of initiative and investment. But when you aren't, you get no credit.

If you did only your core responsibilities and none of the extras, your job should not be in any danger. But if you don't do the core parts, no matter how many extras you do, eventually your job probably will be in danger.
In the long run, you'll be happier and more successful if you are truly engaged by the work you were hired to do, not just by the extracurriculars.
key_insight: Extracurricular contributions only count as signs of initiative when you're already meeting expectations on the core work you were hired to do — otherwise they become evidence of poor prioritization.
